meta.title: GLC Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes-Benz • GLC • 2015–present • 2 Generations • OM651 • OM654 • M276
hero.h1: GLC Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every GLC generation covered — X253 and X254, petrol and diesel, real UK running costs and reliability data from 526+ real enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED]. The UK's most-enquired Mercedes SUV — backed by 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists and real market intelligence.

metrics.ourCall: ✅ The GLC rule: safe at any price — the X254's £28,000–£55,000 value makes replacement trivial; the X253's £14,000–£38,000 value absorbs even the most expensive OM642 recon comfortably
OM654 2.0 diesel — the modern modular diesel that powers the X254 and late X253; materially fewer failure reports than the OM651 predecessor [MB‑VERIFIED]
OM651 2.1 CDI — the earlier X253 diesel with the "Black Death" injector-seal caveat; manageable and preventable, but the OM654 is a clear step forward [MB‑VERIFIED]
X253 GLC 220d (2018–2022) — post-facelift X253 with OM654, 304-enquiry sweet spot, £3,000–£5,000 recon cost against £14,000–£38,000 vehicle value [MB‑VERIFIED]
OM651 injector seal / "Black Death" — 60%+ of X253 diesel enquiries in our data [MB‑VERIFIED]
🏆 Safest SUV Buy — the GLC X254 carries the cleanest ✅ Safe verdict of any Mercedes-Benz SUV in our data

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The GLC is the safest Mercedes-Benz SUV you can buy — the X254 is almost impossible to make a bad decision on, and the X253 post‑2018 with OM654 is a used‑buy bargain that won't punish you at repair time. If you're buying a Mercedes SUV, start here.

generations.subHeadline: The GLC runs in two generations — the first-generation X253 (2015–2022) and the second-generation X254 (2022–present). Both are safe buys, but the dividing line matters for engine choice and value.

cards.title: X253 GLC (2015–2022)
cards.meta: Years: 2015–2022 • Key Engines: OM651 2.1 CDI (pre-2018), OM654 2.0 CDI (post-2018), M274 2.0 turbo petrol, M276 3.0 V6 petrol — 🔥 Most Enquired — 304 enquiries (2025) [MB‑VERIFIED]
cards.verdict: The original GLC set the standard for compact luxury SUVs — and it's held up well. The pre‑2018 OM651 diesel carries the "Black Death" injector‑seal caveat (cheap and preventable, but worth knowing), while the post‑2018 OM654 is materially more reliable. At £14,000–£38,000 vehicle value against £3,000–£5,000 recon cost, replacement makes solid sense even on the most expensive engine [MB‑QUOTE]. Buy a 2018+ X253 with OM654 for the sweet spot, or a well‑maintained pre‑2018 OM651 if the history is documented.

cards.title: X254 GLC (2022–present)
cards.meta: Years: 2022–present • Key Engines: OM654 2.0 diesel (all variants), M254 2.0 turbo petrol, plug‑in hybrid variants — 🔥 Most Enquired — 222 enquiries (2025) [MB‑VERIFIED]
cards.verdict: The new-generation GLC is the safest Mercedes SUV in our data — the OM654‑only diesel range carries none of the OM651's caveats, and £28,000–£55,000 vehicle value against £3,000–£5,000 recon cost makes replacement almost trivial [MB‑QUOTE]. If your budget stretches to a used X254, buy it with confidence. This is the Mercedes SUV that makes the most sense in 2026.

engineDatabase.h2: GLC Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every GLC engine code ever offered in the UK — verified against factory technical specs and cross‑referenced with real 2025 enquiry data.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651 and OM654 ratings: 90% confidence — based on 304+222 [MB‑VERIFIED] enquiries and cross‑referenced with the known OM651 "Black Death" injector‑seal failure pattern and OM654's cleaner record. Petrol variants (M274, M276, M254): Expert‑knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist interviews, not proprietary volume data, because petrol GLC enquiries are lower volume in our 2025 diesel‑dominant dataset [PATTERN DATA]. Data note (internal) — corrections applied: * Correction applied: The X253 GLC's pre‑2018 diesel engine is the OM651 (2.1 CDI), not the OM654 — the OM654 replaced the OM651 in the GLC during the 2018 facelift. The two engines have materially different reliability profiles (OM651: "Black Death" injector‑seal risk; OM654: cleaner record). Split the enquiry data by year to avoid misattributing OM654's reliability to OM651. * Correction applied: The X254 GLC's diesel engine is the OM654 (in both standard and mild‑hybrid forms) — the OM651 was discontinued in 2019 across the Mercedes range. The X254 does not carry the OM651's injector‑seal caveat. * Correction applied: The M274 and M276 petrol engine codes confirmed against factory technical specs — the M276 is the V6 fitted to the GLC 43 AMG (X253), while the X254's petrol range is the M254 (2.0‑litre mild hybrid) and the M139 (AMG). * Verification check: The X254 GLC's M139 engine is the same high‑output 2.0‑litre turbo fitted to the A45 S AMG — 416–421 bhp, making it the most powerful 2.0‑litre production engine ever fitted to a Mercedes SUV. * No further corrections needed — source data matched live verification.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The failures GLC owners actually encounter — ranked by severity and backed by 526+ real UK enquiries.

problems.issue: OM651 "Black Death" Injector Seal Failure (X253, pre‑2018)
problems.description: The defining diesel issue across all OM651‑powered Mercedes models — copper injector seals degrade, allowing combustion gases to leak past and form a thick carbon "tar" around the injector base. Left untreated, the carbon can weld the injector into the cylinder head, turning a £400–£1,200 seal job into a full cylinder‑head repair or engine replacement. Most common on X253 GLC 220d/250d from 2015–2018 with 70,000–100,000 miles. [MB‑VERIFIED]

problems.issue: OM654 AdBlue System Issues (X253 post‑2018, X254)
problems.description: The OM654's AdBlue system is the most common non‑engine failure in our GLC data — typically NOx sensor or heater failure, software‑manageable, £400–£1,200 repair. Not a showstopper, but frustrating. The X254's OM654 has a more refined AdBlue system than the X253's; failure reports are lower on the newer generation. [MB‑VERIFIED]

problems.issue: M276 V6 Petrol Timing Chain Stretch (X253 GLC 43, pre‑2020)
problems.description: The 3.0‑litre V6 in the GLC 43 can suffer timing chain stretch on earlier examples (2015–2019). Chain stretch is typically noticeable as a rattle on cold start. Repair costs £1,500–£2,500; if ignored, valve‑to‑piston contact can occur. Post‑2020 M276 examples are revised and more robust [PATTERN DATA].

problems.issue: M274 2.0 Turbo Petrol Water Pump Failure (X253)
problems.description: The 2.0‑litre turbo petrol in the X253 GLC 200/250/300 — water pump leaks are the most common M274 failure in our data. Typically occurs 60,000–80,000 miles, £400–£800 repair. Not catastrophic, but coolant loss can lead to overheating if ignored [PATTERN DATA].

The OM651's DPF is sensitive to short, cold urban trips — the GLC is often bought as a luxury SUV for city driving, which directly exacerbates this problem. Symptoms: limp mode, fuel‑diluted oil, £400–£1,200 for DPF clean or replacement. The fix is a 30‑minute motorway run at 2,500rpm once a month — but many owners don't know that [MB‑VERIFIED].

problems.issue: M139 Turbocharger Heat Management (X254 GLC 63 S E Performance)
problems.description: The high‑output 2.0‑litre AMG engine in the X254 GLC 63 generates significant under‑bonnet heat — turbocharger oil lines and coolant lines are the weak points. The engine is still relatively new in SUV applications, but early‑adopter reports suggest oil‑line degradation is a real concern at 30,000–50,000 miles [PATTERN DATA].

marketIntelligence.h2: GLC Market Intelligence — What 526+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025

signals.data: 304 enquiries — the most‑enquired Mercedes SUV in our data [MB‑VERIFIED]
222 enquiries — strong demand for the new generation [MB‑VERIFIED]
80%+ of GLC enquiries are diesel (OM651/OM654); petrol enquiries are <20% [MB‑VERIFIED]
60% of X253 enquiries are OM651 (pre‑2018); 40% are OM654 (post‑2018) — but OM654 enquiries are ⬆ Rising as the used market shifts [MB‑VERIFIED]

insights: Interesting insight: The GLC's combined 526 enquiries make it the most‑enquired Mercedes SUV in our 2025 dataset — and the X254's 222 enquiries in its first full year on the used market already outpace every other Mercedes SUV's first‑year volume. Buyers are actively seeking out the second‑generation GLC, and the OM654‑only diesel range is the primary draw.

The X253 GLC's OM651 "Black Death" injector‑seal failure is the single biggest driver of X253 enquiries — but because it's a cheap, preventable fix if caught early, the OM651 is still a viable used‑buy option. The X253's OM654 variant (2018+) has materially fewer failure reports and is the recommended sweet spot.

The GLC's diesel‑dominant enquiry split (80%+ diesel) reflects the UK market's preference for diesel SUVs — but the X254's petrol‑hybrid variants are gaining ground, especially in urban areas with ULEZ restrictions. The M254 mild‑hybrid petrol and M139 AMG variants are low‑volume but growing in interest.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every GLC Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy a post‑2018 X253 with the OM654 diesel, or stretch to an X254 — both carry the ✅ Safe verdict in our data. The pre‑2018 OM651 is a viable used‑buy if the injector seals have been done, but the OM654's cleaner record makes the facelift X253 the clear sweet spot. The GLC is the Mercedes SUV that makes the most financial and engineering sense in 2026. MERCEDES GLC — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: GLC Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every GLC engine — used, reconditioned and rebuilt options, supply-only, with independent labour guidance. All figures [MB-QUOTE].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting a GLC engine typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). AMG variants require specialist tooling and take longer — budget 16–20 hours for M139 work [MB-QUOTE]. The GLC rule — safe at any price: An X253 GLC 220d (OM651) at typical market value of £14,000–£38,000 against a £2,800–£4,800 recon cost sits at 7–34% — comfortably below our 70% threshold [THIRD-PARTY]. An X254 GLC 220d (OM654) at £28,000–£55,000 against £3,000–£5,000 sits at 5–18% — trivially safe [MB-QUOTE]. The honest advice: fix any GLC that needs an engine — the car is worth far more than the work. The only caveat is the M139 AMG — at £6,000–£10,000 recon against a £40,000–£80,000 X254 GLC 63, it's still safe (8–25%), but the specialist pool is smaller and you need to be confident in the workshop doing the work.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a GLC engine typically takes 8–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). AMG variants require specialist tooling and take longer — budget 16–20 hours for M139 work [MB-QUOTE]. The GLC rule — safe at any price: An X253 GLC 220d (OM651) at typical market value of £14,000–£38,000 against a £2,800–£4,800 recon cost sits at 7–34% — comfortably below our 70% threshold [THIRD-PARTY]. An X254 GLC 220d (OM654) at £28,000–£55,000 against £3,000–£5,000 sits at 5–18% — trivially safe [MB-QUOTE]. The honest advice: fix any GLC that needs an engine — the car is worth far more than the work. The only caveat is the M139 AMG — at £6,000–£10,000 recon against a £40,000–£80,000 X254 GLC 63, it's still safe (8–25%), but the specialist pool is smaller and you need to be confident in the workshop doing the work.

engineEvolution.h2: How the GLC Engine Range Evolved — 2015 to Today

eras.keyEngines: OM651 2.1 CDI, M274 2.0 turbo petrol, M276 3.0 V6 (GLC 43)
eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes entered the compact luxury SUV segment with the GLC — replacing the GLK (which was never sold in the UK). The GLC used the MRA platform, shared with the C-Class, and adopted the same engine range.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM651 diesel is the defining engine of this era — but it carries the "Black Death" injector-seal caveat. The M274 petrol is reliable but less common in the UK market (diesel dominates at 80%+ of enquiries) [MB‑VERIFIED].

eras.whyBmwChanged: Emissions‑driven: the OM654 replaced the OM651 across the Mercedes range — it's a cleaner, more modular design that also improved reliability (fewer injector-seal failures, better AdBlue system).
eras.worthKnowing: This is the defining sweet spot. The OM654 is materially more reliable than the OM651 — 2018+ X253 GLC 220d is the best used GLC buy in our data. The facelift also brought MBUX infotainment and better electronics.

eras.keyEngines: OM654 (all diesel variants), M254 2.0 turbo petrol (mild hybrid), M139 2.0 turbo petrol (AMG)
eras.whyBmwChanged: The X254 moved to the second-generation MRA platform — all engines are electrified (mild hybrid or plug-in hybrid), and the OM651 is finally retired. The AMG GLC 63 uses the M139 — the most powerful 2.0‑litre production engine ever fitted to a Mercedes SUV.
eras.worthKnowing: The X254 is the safest Mercedes SUV in our data — the OM654‑only diesel range carries none of the OM651's caveats, and the vehicle value is high enough that replacement cost is almost trivial. The M139 AMG is a specialist option — powerful, but maintenance‑heavy.

profiles.verdict: The GLC is the perfect family SUV — spacious enough for two children and a dog, safe, refined, and with the OM654 diesel, economical. The X253 post‑2018 is the used‑buy sweet spot; the X254 is the aspirational upgrade. Both carry ✅ Safe verdicts — you can buy without worry.

The GLC's diesel‑dominant range is built for motorway miles — 45–55 mpg, comfortable ride, and enough power for A‑road overtakes. The X254's mild‑hybrid OM654 adds 48V assistance for smoother stop‑start and better urban fuel economy. If you're covering 15,000+ miles a year, the GLC is one of the best SUVs you can buy.

If you want a premium SUV but don't need the size of the GLE or the complexity of the G‑Class, the GLC is the sweet spot. It's cheaper to buy and run than the GLE, more spacious than the GLA, and holds its value better than both. The X254 is the aspirational choice; the X253 post‑2018 is the value play.

The OM654 diesel is Euro 6d‑compliant and ULEZ‑friendly — but the petrol and plug‑in hybrid variants are the cleaner choices if you're driving into London or Birmingham daily. The X254's M254 mild‑hybrid petrol is the best of both worlds — ULEZ‑friendly, economical, and no AdBlue system to manage.

This is the GLC's strongest profile. The X254 is the safest Mercedes SUV in our data — the OM654's reliability, the platform's durability, and the vehicle's strong retained values make it a genuine keeper. A 2022+ X254 GLC 220d with full service history is the Mercedes SUV that will still be on the road in 2035 with 150,000 miles on it.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My GLC Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The GLC's economics are straightforward — replacement cost is almost always well within vehicle value. If your X253 GLC has an OM651 "Black Death" injector‑seal failure (the most common GLC diesel problem in our data), a £400–£1,200 repair is a no‑brainer [MB‑VERIFIED]. If the engine has suffered terminal bottom‑end damage (rare on both OM651 and OM654), the repair‑vs‑replace decision is still in the GLC's favour — the car's value absorbs the cost comfortably. Use our calculator to get a personalised verdict.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost figure on this page is drawn from 526+ real UK GLC enquiries in 2025 [MB‑VERIFIED] — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: When a GLC's replacement cost exceeds 70% of the car's value, we say so. The honest truth? On the GLC, replacement almost never exceeds the threshold — but we'd still tell you if it did. That's the difference between a knowledge base and a sales page.

signals.title: Vetted Mercedes
signals.text: Benz Specialists — Our network of 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists includes workshops that work on GLC OM651 injector‑seal failures and OM654 AdBlue issues every week — they're vetted specifically for diesel‑injector diagnosis and AdBlue system work, not generalists who happen to take on Mercedes jobs.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
